subject: ap-chemistry
title: Enthalpy of Solution: LiCl vs. NaCl
stimulus:
A student investigates the enthalpy of solution, $\Delta H_{soln}$, for two alkali metal halides, LiCl and NaCl. In addition
to the salts, the student has access to a calorimeter, a balance with a precision of ±0.1 g, and a thermometer with
a precision of ±0.1°C.

parts.prompt:
To measure $\Delta H_{soln}$ for LiCl, the student adds 100.0 g of water initially at 15.0°C to a calorimeter and adds
10.0 g of LiCl(s), stirring to dissolve. After the LiCl dissolves completely, the maximum temperature
reached by the solution is 35.6°C.
(i) Calculate the magnitude of the heat absorbed by the solution during the dissolution process, assuming
that the specific heat capacity of the solution is 4.18 J/(g·°C). Include units with your answer.
(ii) Determine the value of $\Delta H_{soln}$ for LiCl in kJ/mol.

scoring_points.description: For correct heat calculation using total solution mass: 1 point

required_elements: $q = mc\Delta T = (110.0\text{ g})(4.18\text{ J/g·°C})(20.6°\text{C}) = 9480\text{ J} = 9.48\text{ kJ}$

wrong_examples: Using only 100.0 g (water mass only) — the total solution mass includes both water and dissolved LiCl.
common_traps: The total mass of the solution = mass of water + mass of dissolved salt.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct setup. 1 point is earned for the correct answer with units.

sample_responses.commentary: Uses the total solution mass (100.0 g water + 10.0 g LiCl = 110.0 g) and correct $\Delta T$, earning the setup point.
Uses only the mass of water (100.0 g) rather than the total solution mass, which is the most common error for this point.

scoring_points.description: For correct molar enthalpy with correct sign: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{LiCl}) = 10.0/42.39 = 0.2359\text{ mol}$
$\Delta H_{soln} = -9480\text{ J} / 0.2359\text{ mol} = -40200\text{ J/mol} = -40.2\text{ kJ/mol}$
(Negative because the solution temperature increased → exothermic dissolution.)

wrong_examples: Reporting a positive ΔH$_{soln}$ — the temperature increased, indicating heat was released to solution, so ΔH$_{soln}$ is negative.
Not dividing by moles — must calculate per mole.
common_traps: Sign convention: temperature rise → exothermic reaction → negative ΔH$_{soln}$. The solution absorbed heat (q > 0) but the reaction released it.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the number of moles of LiCl. 1 point is earned for the correct $\Delta H_{soln}$ and the correct sign.

sample_responses.response: $n(\text{LiCl}) = \frac{10.0\text{ g}}{42.39\text{ g/mol}} = 0.236\text{ mol}$; $\Delta H_{soln} = \frac{-9.47\text{ kJ}}{0.236\text{ mol}} = -40.1\text{ kJ/mol}$
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly calculates moles of LiCl and divides the negative heat (exothermic, temperature rose) by moles to obtain the correct negative molar enthalpy.
Correct magnitude but wrong sign — since the flask warms up, the dissolution releases heat to the solution ($\Delta H_{soln}$ is negative, exothermic).

parts.prompt: Write the complete electron configuration for the Na$^+$ ion in the ground state.

scoring_points.description: For the correct electron configuration of Na$^+$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Na$^+$ has lost one electron (from 3s), leaving 10 electrons: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ (isoelectronic with Ne).

wrong_examples: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ 3s$^1$ — that is the configuration of the neutral Na atom, not Na$^+$.
common_traps: Na$^+$ forms by losing the 3s$^1$ electron from neutral Na. Na$^+$ has 10 electrons: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the complete correct configuration.

sample_responses.commentary: Na$^+$ has 10 electrons (Na loses its 3s$^1$ electron to form Na$^+$); the complete configuration 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ is correct.
This is the configuration of neutral Na (11 electrons), not Na$^+$ which has lost the 3s electron.

parts.prompt: Using principles of atomic structure, explain why the Na$^+$ ion is larger than the Li$^+$ ion.
parts.scoring_notes: Na$^+$ has its outermost electrons in the 2s/2p subshells (n = 2), while Li$^+$ has its outermost electrons in the 1s subshell (n = 1). Higher principal quantum number → electrons are, on average, farther from the nucleus → larger ion.

scoring_points.description: For explaining size difference in terms of principal energy level: 1 point

required_elements: Na$^+$ has electrons in a higher principal energy level (n = 2) than Li$^+$ (n = 1).
Electrons in higher principal levels are farther from the nucleus on average → larger radius.
alternatives.correct_example: Li$^+$: 1s$^2$ — outermost electrons are in n = 1. Na$^+$: 1s$^2$ 2s$^2$ 2p$^6$ — outermost electrons are in n = 2. Electrons in higher principal energy levels have a larger average distance from the nucleus. Therefore Na$^+$ (n = 2 outermost) is larger than Li$^+$ (n = 1 outermost).

wrong_examples: Saying Na has more protons — the question is about Na$^+$ vs. Li$^+$; proton count alone doesn't explain the size trend between these isoelectronic-like comparison.
common_traps: Size increases as principal quantum number increases. Both Li$^+$ and Na$^+$ are cations; the key difference is the number of electron shells (n = 1 vs. n = 2).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for a correct explanation based on occupied principal energy levels.

sample_responses.response: The valence electrons in the Na$^+$ ion are in a higher principal energy level (n = 2) than the valence electrons in the Li$^+$ ion (n = 1). Electrons in higher principal energy levels are, on average, farther from the nucleus.
sample_responses.commentary: Directly references principal energy level difference; this is the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: Na$^+$ has more protons so it pulls electrons in more tightly, making it smaller.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses the trend — more protons would pull electrons in closer, making the ion smaller, not larger. Must reference the higher principal energy level (n = 2) of Na$^+$.

parts.prompt: Which salt, LiCl or NaCl, has the greater lattice enthalpy? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: LiCl has the greater lattice enthalpy. Li$^+$ is smaller than Na$^+$, so the interionic distance between Li$^+$ and Cl$^-$ is shorter. Shorter distance → stronger Coulombic attraction (lattice energy ∝ 1/r) → greater lattice enthalpy (more energy required to separate the ions).

scoring_points.description: For LiCl with correct Coulombic/size reasoning: 1 point

required_elements: Li$^+$ is smaller than Na$^+$, so the interionic distance is shorter in LiCl.
Shorter distance → stronger Coulombic attraction → greater lattice enthalpy.
alternatives.correct_example: LiCl has the greater lattice enthalpy. The lattice enthalpy is related to the strength of Coulombic attractions between cations and anions ($E \propto q_1q_2/r$). Li$^+$ (ionic radius 76 pm) is smaller than Na$^+$ (102 pm), so the Li$^+$—Cl$^-$ distance is shorter. Shorter distance → stronger attractive force → more energy required to separate the ions → greater lattice enthalpy.

wrong_examples: NaCl has greater lattice enthalpy — larger ions are farther apart, giving weaker attractions.
common_traps: Greater lattice enthalpy = stronger ionic bonds. Smaller cation → shorter interionic distance → stronger bonds → higher lattice enthalpy.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for the correct choice and justification.

sample_responses.response: LiCl. Because the Li$^+$ ion is smaller than the Na$^+$ ion, the Coulombic attractions between ions in LiCl are stronger than in NaCl. This results in a greater lattice enthalpy.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies LiCl, cites the smaller Li$^+$ radius, and links it to stronger Coulombic attraction — the CB model answer.

sample_responses.response: NaCl, because Na$^+$ is larger and its electrons are more spread out.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect choice. Larger ion → greater interionic distance → weaker Coulombic attraction → lower lattice enthalpy, not greater.

parts.prompt:
Below is a representation of a portion of a crystal of LiCl. Identify the ions in the representation by writing
the appropriate formulas (Li$^+$ or Cl$^-$) in the boxes below.
parts.scoring_notes: In a LiCl crystal (rock-salt structure), the larger spheres are Cl$^-$ and the smaller spheres are Li$^+$. Must correctly assign the smaller ions as Li$^+$ and the larger ions as Cl$^-$.

scoring_points.description: For correctly labeling larger spheres as Cl$^-$ and smaller as Li$^+$: 1 point

required_elements: Smaller spheres = Li$^+$; larger spheres = Cl$^-$.
alternatives.correct_example: In an ionic crystal, the ionic radius of Cl$^-$ (181 pm) is much larger than Li$^+$ (76 pm). The larger spheres in the crystal diagram represent Cl$^-$ ions and the smaller spheres represent Li$^+$ ions.

wrong_examples: Swapping the labels — Cl$^-$ is always significantly larger than Li$^+$.
common_traps: Anions are generally larger than cations. Cl$^-$ has gained an electron and is large; Li$^+$ has lost an electron and is small.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for both identifications.

sample_responses.response: Larger spheres: Cl$^-$; Smaller spheres: Li$^+$
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that the anion Cl$^-$ is substantially larger than the cation Li$^+$ in the rock-salt structure, earning the point.

sample_responses.response: Larger spheres: Li$^+$; Smaller spheres: Cl$^-$
sample_responses.commentary: Reversed — Cl$^-$ (ionic radius 181 pm) is much larger than Li$^+$ (76 pm); anions are generally larger than cations.

parts.prompt:
The lattice enthalpy of LiCl is positive, indicating that it takes energy to break the ions apart in LiCl.
However, the dissolution of LiCl in water is an exothermic process. Identify all particle-particle interactions
that contribute significantly to the dissolution process being exothermic. For each interaction, include the
particles that interact and the specific type of intermolecular force between those particles.
parts.scoring_notes: The dissolution process is exothermic because the hydration energy released exceeds the lattice enthalpy input. Key interactions: (1) Li$^+$ ions and water molecules — ion–dipole interactions (strong because Li$^+$ is small and highly charged). (2) Cl$^-$ ions and water molecules — ion–dipole interactions (O–H of water oriented toward Cl$^-$). These two sets of ion–dipole interactions release sufficient energy to overcome the lattice enthalpy and drive exothermic dissolution.

scoring_points.description: For identifying Li$^+$–water interaction as ion–dipole: 1 point

required_elements: Ion–dipole interaction (the partial negative O of water is attracted to the positive Li$^+$).
alternatives.correct_example: Li$^+$ ions interact with water molecules via ion–dipole interactions: the partial negative charge on the oxygen of water is attracted to the Li$^+$ cation. These interactions release energy as Li$^+$ becomes hydrated.

wrong_examples: Saying hydrogen bonds — Li$^+$ does not form hydrogen bonds.
common_traps: Ion–solvent interactions are called ion–dipole forces. Both cations and anions undergo ion–dipole interactions with polar water.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for identifying the particles that interact (Li$^+$ ions and polar water molecules; Cl$^-$ ions and polar water molecules).

sample_responses.response: Li$^+$ ions and polar water molecules interact via ion–dipole interactions.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly names both the particles and the interaction type for the cation; earns the particle-identification point.

sample_responses.response: Li$^+$ and water form hydrogen bonds.
sample_responses.commentary: Li$^+$ is a cation, not a hydrogen-bond donor or acceptor in the traditional sense; the correct term is ion–dipole interaction.

scoring_points.description: For identifying Cl$^-$–water interaction as ion–dipole: 1 point

required_elements: Ion–dipole interaction (hydrogen atoms of water oriented toward the Cl$^-$ anion).
alternatives.correct_example: Cl$^-$ ions interact with water molecules via ion–dipole interactions: the partially positive H atoms of water are oriented toward the negatively charged Cl$^-$ ion. These interactions release energy as Cl$^-$ becomes hydrated.

wrong_examples: Attributing Cl$^-$–water interaction to hydrogen bonding — while Cl$^-$ can accept H-bonds, the primary force here is ion–dipole.
common_traps: Both Li$^+$ and Cl$^-$ must be separately identified as forming ion–dipole interactions with water.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point is earned for correctly identifying the type of interaction as ion–dipole forces between the ions and polar water molecules.

sample_responses.response: Both Li$^+$ and Cl$^-$ ions interact with polar water molecules through ion–dipole interactions.
sample_responses.commentary: Names both ions, specifies polar water as the partner, and correctly labels the interaction as ion–dipole.

sample_responses.response: Cl$^-$ and water molecules interact via London dispersion forces.
sample_responses.commentary: London dispersion forces are for neutral, nonpolar molecules; an ion interacting with a polar molecule creates an ion–dipole force.

scoring_points.description: For explaining why these hydration interactions make dissolution exothermic: 1 point

required_elements: The energy released by ion–dipole (hydration) interactions exceeds the energy required to overcome the lattice enthalpy.
Net energy is released → exothermic dissolution.
alternatives.correct_example: The energy released when Li$^+$ and Cl$^-$ form ion–dipole interactions with water molecules (hydration energy) is greater than the lattice enthalpy needed to separate the ions. Because the hydration energy exceeds the lattice energy, the net enthalpy of dissolution is negative (exothermic).

wrong_examples: Not explaining why these interactions cause exothermic dissolution — must connect to the energy balance.
common_traps: Exothermic dissolution: $\Delta H_{soln} = \Delta H_{lattice}(+) + \Delta H_{hydration}(-) < 0$. The hydration energy must exceed the lattice enthalpy.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Part (f) is worth 2 points: 1 point for identifying the particles that interact, 1 point for correctly identifying the type of interaction. The connection to the exothermic nature of the dissolution is implicit in the identification of the ion-dipole interactions.

sample_responses.response: The energy released by the ion–dipole interactions between the ions and water (hydration energy) exceeds the energy needed to break apart the LiCl lattice, so the net process is exothermic.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly connects hydration energy release to the overall exothermic dissolution by comparing it to the lattice enthalpy.

sample_responses.response: The dissolution is exothermic because LiCl breaks into ions.
sample_responses.commentary: Breaking ionic bonds requires energy (endothermic); the dissolution is exothermic because the hydration energy released exceeds the lattice energy input. The response does not identify the specific particle interactions.
